refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe remote operation by tele-operators.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety margins. The p95 percentile metric ensures consistent performance under variable network conditions rather than optimistic average-case assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit latency budgeting and verification.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (command transmission → vehicle sensor acquisition → telemetry reception at operator console) across representative network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) using instrumented test harnesses. Collect p95 statistics over ≥1000 round-trip samples per condition.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation subsystem shall acquire and encode vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor data) with acquisition-to-transmission latency ≤ 40 ms.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The network link shall transport telemetry and tasking messages with transmission latency ≤ 80 ms (p95) under agreed conditions.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation subsystem shall decode received tasking commands and apply them to vehicle control with reception-to-actuation latency ≤ 30 ms.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The safety services shall monitor teleoperation latency and enforce safe operational boundaries when latency exceeds thresholds.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement low-latency telemetry pipeline with hardware-timestamped sensor acquisition, edge compression, and priority-queued network transmission; implement command decoding with interrupt-driven actuation.
DP-B: Deploy dedicated low-latency network overlay (e.g., QoS-prioritized VPN or private network slice) with latency SLA guarantees; implement redundant communication paths with automatic failover.
DP-C: Implement adaptive latency compensation via predictive state extrapolation at operator console and command buffering at vehicle; use machine learning to anticipate operator intent.
DP-D: Combine DP-A (hardware-optimized pipeline) + DP-B (network SLA) + DP-D (safety monitoring) as integrated solution with latency budgeting across all three domains.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Integrated latency control architecture combining (1) hardware-optimized telemetry/tasking pipeline with deterministic timing (DP-A), (2) network-layer SLA enforcement with QoS and redundancy (DP-B), and (3) safety-critical latency monitoring with operational mode constraints (DP-C). Latency budget allocation: vehicle acquisition/actuation 70 ms, network transport 80 ms (p95), with 0 ms margin reserved for safety threshold enforcement.

potential_sources: Network latency (DP-B) couples to vehicle control responsiveness (DP-A); mitigation via explicit latency budget and failsafe modes.
Safety monitoring (DP-C) couples to operational mode transitions; mitigation via state machine with hysteresis to prevent oscillation.
Predictive compensation (DP-C) couples to sensor accuracy; mitigation via validation against actual state feedback.
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are manageable via explicit interface contracts (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) and latency budgeting. No circular dependencies detected.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network conditions remain within agreed envelope (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss); risk: unplanned network degradation could violate p95 SLA.
ASSUMPTION: Vehicle sensor acquisition hardware supports ≤40 ms latency; risk: legacy sensors may not meet timing requirements.
ASSUMPTION: Tele-operator console can process and display telemetry at ≥10 Hz; risk: operator display lag could negate communication latency improvements.
RISK: Predictive compensation (DP-C) may introduce instability if extrapolation model diverges from actual vehicle state.
RISK: Safety threshold enforcement (DP-C) may trigger false positives under transient network congestion, reducing availability.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly separate safety-critical latency monitoring (DP-C) into a distinct, independently verifiable subsystem with clear failure modes (e.g., graceful degradation to manual control vs. hard stop). Add explicit network SLA contract with provider and define fallback operational modes if SLA is violated.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns end-to-end teleoperation latency control, including command reception, tasking decoding, telemetry encoding, and latency monitoring. Interfaces with Vehicle (via Tasking output, Telemetry input), Network Link (via Telemetry/Tasking transport), and Safety Services (via SafetyAlert input for latency-triggered constraints).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, timestamps) from Vehicle module; used to populate operator console display and validate latency budgets.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Sends decoded operator commands (movement, tool actuation, mode changes) to Vehicle module; includes command timestamp for latency tracking.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Transports Telemetry and Tasking messages over agreed network; Teleoperation module monitors transport latency and reports violations to Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives latency threshold violations and operational constraints from Safety Services; triggers mode transitions (e.g., autonomous hold, manual fallback) when latency exceeds safe limits.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation module is the natural owner of end-to-end latency control. All latency-critical logic (command decoding, telemetry encoding, timing instrumentation) is contained within this module. Minimal diffusion: only external interfaces are Telemetry, Tasking, Network Link, and SafetyAlert.

Vehicle module owns sensor acquisition and actuation, which contribute to latency budget (70 ms allocated). However, Vehicle should not own end-to-end latency monitoring or network-layer control; doing so would couple Vehicle to network infrastructure and safety policies, violating separation of concerns.

Safety Services owns latency threshold enforcement and operational mode constraints (SafetyAlert output). However, Safety Services should not own the telemetry/tasking pipeline itself; that would create circular dependency with Teleoperation and diffuse safety logic across multiple modules.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container but should not own detailed latency control logic; that would prevent modular testing and reuse of Teleoperation subsystem.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module contains all latency-critical logic (command decoding, telemetry encoding, timing instrumentation, latency budgeting) and owns the end-to-end latency control DP. Vehicle and Safety Services provide inputs (Telemetry, SafetyAlert) but do not own latency control logic. This assignment maximizes cohesion and minimizes diffusion.
containment_risks: RISK: Teleoperation module may become a bottleneck if it must serialize all telemetry/tasking processing; mitigation: implement multi-threaded or event-driven architecture with lock-free queues.

RISK: Teleoperation module depends on Vehicle module's sensor acquisition latency; if Vehicle cannot meet 40 ms budget, Teleoperation cannot meet overall 150 ms target; mitigation: establish explicit latency SLA with Vehicle module and include in acceptance criteria.

RISK: Teleoperation module depends on Network Link's transport latency; if network SLA is violated, Teleoperation cannot compensate; mitigation: implement graceful degradation (e.g., autonomous hold) when network latency exceeds threshold.
RISK: SafetyAlert interface may introduce additional latency if Safety Services processing is slow; mitigation: implement SafetyAlert as high-priority interrupt with bounded processing time.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish explicit latency SLA contracts between Teleoperation and Vehicle (sensor acquisition ≤40 ms), Network Link (transport ≤80 ms p95), and Safety Services (SafetyAlert processing ≤5 ms). Define fallback operational modes (e.g., autonomous hold, manual control) when any SLA is violated. Implement latency instrumentation and monitoring as first-class feature in Teleoperation module, not as afterthought.

fr_dp_gate.reason: Selected DP-D (integrated latency control) directly addresses FR-NGHE-008 by combining hardware optimization, network SLA, and safety monitoring. DP-D is traceable to all sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.4) and includes explicit latency budgeting (70 ms vehicle + 80 ms network = 150 ms total). No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Latency monitoring logic is concentrated in Teleoperation module; if Teleoperation module becomes too large, consider extracting latency monitoring into a separate utility library (but keep ownership in Teleoperation).
Network Link module must provide latency metrics (e.g., timestamp on send/receive); ensure Network Link interface contract includes these metrics.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module is the clear owner of end-to-end latency control. Vehicle and Safety Services provide inputs but do not own latency logic. Containment score of 95 for Teleoperation is justified; diffusion is minimal (only 4 external interfaces). No diffusion to NGHE Solution or other modules.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via instrumented testing: measure end-to-end latency (command transmission → telemetry reception) under representative network conditions. Collect p95 statistics over ≥1000 samples. Verify latency budgets at each stage (Vehicle, Network Link, Teleoperation). Verify SafetyAlert triggers when latency exceeds thresholds.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns end-to-end latency control and must meet p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion. Vehicle module must meet 40 ms sensor acquisition budget. Network Link must meet 80 ms transport budget. Safety Services must enforce latency thresholds via SafetyAlert. All allocations are traceable to sub-FRs.

recommended_revisions: Establish explicit latency SLA contracts between Teleoperation and Vehicle, Network Link, and Safety Services. Include SLA metrics in interface specifications.
Define fallback operational modes (e.g., autonomous hold, manual control) when any SLA is violated. Include mode transition logic in Teleoperation module.
Implement latency instrumentation as first-class feature in Teleoperation module, not as afterthought. Include timestamp logging, latency histograms, and p95 calculation.
Validate that operator console display latency (not included in current DP) does not exceed 50 ms; if it does, overall system latency may exceed 150 ms even if Teleoperation module meets its budget.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-008 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.4) that address distinct latency domains (vehicle acquisition, network transport, command actuation, safety monitoring). Each sub-FR requires further decomposition to define detailed DPs and IMs. Proceeding to next level is necessary to allocate latency budgets, define interface contracts, and assign ownership to specific modules.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation subsystem shall acquire and encode vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor data) with acquisition-to-transmission latency ≤ 40 ms.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The network link shall transport telemetry and tasking messages with transmission latency ≤ 80 ms (p95) under agreed conditions.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation subsystem shall decode received tasking commands and apply them to vehicle control with reception-to-actuation latency ≤ 30 ms.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The safety services shall monitor teleoperation latency and enforce safe operational boundaries when latency exceeds thresholds.

assumptions: Network conditions remain within agreed envelope (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss).
Vehicle sensor acquisition hardware supports ≤40 ms latency.
Tele-operator console can process and display telemetry at ≥10 Hz.
Safety Services can process SafetyAlert and enforce constraints within 5 ms.
Operator console display latency is ≤50 ms (not included in current DP but critical to overall system latency).
open_risks: Unplanned network degradation could violate p95 SLA; mitigation: establish network SLA contract with provider and define fallback modes.
Predictive compensation (DP-C) may introduce instability if extrapolation model diverges from actual vehicle state; mitigation: validate extrapolation against actual state feedback.
Safety threshold enforcement may trigger false positives under transient network congestion; mitigation: implement hysteresis in threshold detection.
Operator console display latency may exceed 50 ms, causing overall system latency to exceed 150 ms; mitigation: measure and optimize operator console latency as part of acceptance testing.
evidence_needed: Network SLA contract specifying transport latency, jitter, and packet loss under agreed conditions.
Vehicle sensor acquisition latency specification (≤40 ms) from Vehicle module owner.
Operator console display latency measurement (target ≤50 ms).
Instrumented test results showing end-to-end latency distribution (p95 ≤ 150 ms) under representative network conditions.
Safety Services latency specification (SafetyAlert processing ≤5 ms).
Fallback operational mode specifications (e.g., autonomous hold, manual control) when latency exceeds thresholds.
